title: The Next Chapter of Big Data in Finance
abstract: The second special issue on big data in finance showcases advancements in research related to data of large size, high dimension, and complex structure since the first NBER/RFS big data conference. The papers published in this next chapter address some questions that were proposed in the initial special issue in 2021. Other papers are more directly connected to recent developments in the markets. We discuss some new research directions, following on the papers published here. They include evaluating market microstructure reforms, understanding medium-frequency trading, improving missing data imputations, and deepening data valuation. We look forward to more developments to follow.

article.1. What Is Included in This Special Issu:
Issue Section: Article

The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) and theReview of Financial Studies(RFS) have held joint conferences on “Big Data in Finance” since March of 2019.1In July 2021, the RFS published a special issue on this topic, based largely on papers from the early editions of this conference series. We are now happy to publish the second special issue—“The Next Chapter of Big Data in Finance”—reflecting more recent papers emerging from this joint initiative. In this introduction, we aim to reflect on the progress of big data in finance in this new batch of research and how it extends the directions of research we identified earlier. We also discuss more opportunities for future developments.

A natural starting point for evaluating the progress in research from this initiative is to go back to the three-pronged definition of big data in finance, put forward in our introduction to the previous special issue (Goldstein, Spatt, and Ye 2021). There, we discussed how big data manifests in large size, high dimension, and complex structure:2

Large size: This feature means that data are large in an absolute or relative sense. A natural example of absolute size is transaction-level market microstructure data. In a relative sense, big data is defined relative to the best existing “small data.” For example,O’Hara, Yao, and Ye (2014)found that trade and quote (TAQ) data truncated trades of fewer than 100 shares. Therefore, TAQ data is considered small and induces sample selection bias. It is important to overcome this sample selection bias by using larger datasets.

High dimension: This second feature means that the data have many variables relative to the sample size. The large number of variables may arise naturally. For example,Chinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019)used the minute-by-minute returns of 2,000 other stocks to predict a stock’s one-minute-ahead return in rolling 30-minute estimation windows. Here, the number of predictors is significantly larger than the number of observations (30). The literature on predicting monthly returns usually has fewer predictors, but the nonlinear interactions among variables lead to the high dimensionality of the functional forms.Gu, Kelly, and Xiu (2020)show that the best-performing return-predicting method accounts for these nonlinear interactions.

Complex structure: Unstructured data are data that are not in the traditional row-column format. Examples of unstructured data include text, pictures, audio, and video. These data types are often high-dimensional by nature. The first step in analyzing them is usually to transform the unstructured data into structured data.

In discussing the papers that appear in the current special issue, we will go back to this definition and place them in the relevant bucket of the above three features.Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)andBartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) exemplify the use of data of large size.Aleti and Bollerslev (2025),Freyberger et al. (2025), andBryzgalova et al. (2025)address problems that arise with the high dimensionality of data.Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)pursue different avenues to utilize data of complex structure. A seventh paper in this special issue,Farboodi et al. (2025), takes on a fundamental question—the valuation of financial data—using a theory-guided sufficient-statistic approach.

It is interesting to note that a couple of the papers in this special issue are strongly related to questions put forward as promising for future research in our introduction to the previous special issue. Indeed, inGoldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021), we asked, “What is the price of data?”Farboodi et al. (2025)take some initial steps to tackle this problem. We also wrote that a promising line of research is bridging the gap between studies focusing on a monthly horizon or longer and studies on high-frequency trading focusing on horizons below a second. Traders who apply machine-learning techniques are often faster than most traditional funds but slower than high-frequency traders. This medium horizon is still largely outside the radar of the academic finance literature, butAleti and Bollerslev (2025)contribute to the literature by studying the stochastic discount factor (SDF) at a 15-minute horizon.

Other papers in this special issue address research questions prompted by new developments in financial markets. For example,Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)study fractional trading, which emerged recently. Taken together, papers in this special issue suggest that research with big data and the methodologies it brings will continue along two paths: addressing long-standing questions and responding to new events. These are of course related, as new events sometimes helps us understand long-standing questions, as is the case withDa, Fang, and Lin (2025), who shed new light on trading frenzies. After describing the papers in the special issue, we discuss some new directions for research that are both related to the directions suggested in our previous introduction and prompted by the papers published here.
In this section, we review the contributions of the papers in this special issue, using the introduction of our previous special issue as the benchmark.

1.1. Large size

The previous special issue called TAQ data a small dataset becauseO’Hara, Yao, and Ye (2014)found truncation bias in the dataset. As regulators exempted odd lots from reporting requirements, informed traders began using algorithms to slice and dice their orders into trades of fewer than 100 shares to escape reporting requirements. Their paper prompted regulators to include odd-lot trades in reporting requirements. However, this change is not the end of the story.

First, starting in 2019, several brokers began allowing stock trading in increments as small as a penny.Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)study fractional trading, which is the ability to trade less than a whole share. Second, although regulators have included odd-lot trades in the TAQ data, the odd-lot quotes are still invisible.Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) use proprietary data to study the missing odd-lot quotes and find that they contain private information. These two papers are included in this special issue.

1.1.1. Fractional trades

Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)study how the ability to trade less than a whole share affects retail activity and return patterns of high-priced stocks. As the median account balance of a Robinhood investor is only $240 (Securities and Exchange Commission 2021),Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)hypothesize that fractional trading (FT) encourages entry into high-priced stocks by investors with binding capital constraints. They proxy for fractional trading using off-exchange one-share trades based on the “rounding-up rule” detailed inBartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2024).3

Indeed,Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)find that after brokers introduced FT, high-priced stocks experienced significantly larger increases in off-exchange one-share trades relative to low-priced stocks. They observe that this effect is concentrated in stocks with lower institutional ownership. The pandemic also amplified FT.Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)conjecture that one possible reason is that people needed to stay home and simultaneously received stimulus checks.

Next,Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)show that when coordinated during attention-grabbing events, FT exerts significant price pressure on high-priced stocks. After the FT introduction, high-priced stocks experienced a larger increase in off-exchange one-share trades, social media discussions, and price pressure than their low-priced counterparts around attention-grabbing events. These tiny trades are likely uninformed, as high-priced stocks tend to experience more price reversals relative to low-priced stocks after FT introduction.

Finally,Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)find that FT contributes to meme-stock-like trading frenzies and fuels price bubbles. The likelihood of a high-price stock experiencing a bubble increased by 21% from the pre-FT period to the post-FT period compared to low-priced stocks. They argue that the formation of these bubbles follows the feedback mechanism described byGoldstein, Ozdenoren, and Yuan (2013): speculators, including FT traders, drive up the price of meme stocks like GameStop, which attracts capital providers by suggesting improved firm fundamentals. This cycle enhances firm valuations and encourages further speculation.

The comparison betweenDa, Fang, and Lin (2025)and previous literature on trade size reveals an interesting cycle influenced by technology and regulation. In the 1960s and 1970s, the smallest trades were considered the least informative, a view rooted in the odd-lot theory outlined byMalkiel (1981, p. 140), which recommended trading against the smallest trades because they are more likely to come from retail traders.Barclay and Warner (1993)discuss the benefits of breaking large orders into smaller pieces, but they note that humans have physical limits to slicing trades into very small pieces. This constraint led them to find that the most informed trades were typically around 500–999 shares. Advancements in technology altered this dynamic.O’Hara, Yao, and Ye (2014)found that the smallest trades became the most informative, often executed by sophisticated traders using unreported odd lots to conceal their strategies. After the SEC required odd-lot trades to be reported after December 9, 2013,Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)now observe a return to the initial belief that the smallest trades are less informative and more likely to originate from retail traders. This cyclical pattern indicates that a promising line of research is to examine how technology and regulations influence other established market behaviors.

1.1.2. Odd-lot quotes

While all trades must be reported, quotes for fewer than 100 shares remain invisible to the public. Consequently, the term “National Best Bid and Offer” (NBBO) is misleading because it relies on quotes derived from “round lots.”Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) delve into the significance of odd-lot quotes for liquidity and price discovery.

Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) find that odd lots significantly enhance liquidity, especially for high-priced stocks. They categorize stocks into five groups based on price, with Group 5 encompassing stocks priced above $250. They discover that the spread for odd lots is narrower than the NBBO spread across all stock groups, with Group 5 showing an improvement of up to 26%. Moreover, superior odd-lot quotes are present 42% of the time for Group 5 stocks. They further demonstrate that odd-lot quotes offer substantial depth; conditioned on superior quotes, inside depth amounts to nearly 15% of the displayed NBBO depth. Lastly,Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) argue that the missing odd-lot quotes remain significant under the smaller lot sizes proposed by theSEC (2020).

Next,Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) show the importance of odd-lot quotes to price discovery. After they show that odd-lot quotes often precede round-lot quotes, they examine how well odd-lot metrics predict whether a stock’s price will be higher in one minute. Their test comes from a series of horse races between two sets of models. The first model is the securities information processors (SIP) model, which includes only round-lot quotations. The second is the odd-lot (OL) model, which adds odd-lot quotation data from the proprietary feeds.

Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) run their first horse race based on a linear regression. While most SIP variables are insignificant, two variables constructed from odd-lot data show strong positive correlations with one-minute returns. They also find that the OL model’sR2is nearly six times that of the SIP model, although the overall fit of both models remains low.Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) then run their second horse race using XGBoost, a supervised machine-learning method, to investigate return predictability further. They find strong return predictability for both the SIP and OL models, particularly for the OL model. However, once they include the transaction costs, the return from the SIP model becomes negligible, while the average daily return from the OL model can be as high as 1.36%.

“Big data” can refer to (i) new data or (ii) using new statistical methods, such as machine learning, to analyze existing data.Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) demonstrate that both dimensions are crucial. Compared to linear regression, XGBoost significantly improves return predictions for both the SIP and OL models. Additionally, they show that proprietary data play a vital role in their applications. Without proprietary data, XGBoost cannot generate a profitable strategy after adjusting for transaction costs. The importance of OL data in generating returns explains why proprietary odd-lot information is sold at a significant price. In this context, their paper is related the one byFarboodi et al. (2025)in this special issue, which values proprietary data.

1.2. High dimensionality

Goldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021)mentioned that a key characteristic of big data is its high dimensionality, where the number of predictors is larger than the number of observations. High dimensionality naturally occurs in the paper byChinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019), who aim to predict the one-minute-ahead return of a stock in rolling 30-minute estimation windows using lagged minute-by-minute returns of the stock and over 2,000 other stocks listed in New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). Here, the number of predictors far exceeds the number of observations (30).

Goldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021)also comment on high dimensionality in functional forms. If the impact of certain variables is highly nonlinear or involves interaction terms, the dimensionality can be high even if the number of predictors is not (in machine learning, this is known as basis expansion). In this special issue,Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)provide a nice example of high dimensionality in functional forms using neural networks to approximate the SDF’s unknown functional form at the 15-minute horizon.

This special issue also includes two papers on missing data. As the number of predictors increases, the number of observations with complete data can decrease exponentially. Therefore, missing data can create a challenge even when the dimensionality is not excessively high.Freyberger et al. (2025)find that only 10% of observations have no missing values among the 82 characteristics defined byChen and Zimmermann (2022). Similarly,Bryzgalova et al. (2025)report that less than 30% of their sample, which includes 45 characteristics, is complete. Both papers find that two traditional methods of handling missing data, deleting observations with missing characteristics and imputing missing characteristics using their cross-sectional means, produce unsatisfactory results. Each paper then proposes a method to address the issue.

1.2.1. High-frequency returns

Chinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019)investigate minute-level returns of individual stocks, whereasAleti and Bollerslev (2025)focus on 15-minute returns of the SDF.Chinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019)deal with high dimensionality in variables, whereasAleti and Bollerslev (2025)deal with high dimensionality in functional forms. Despite their differences, both studies follow a two-step approach. The first step employs machine-learning methods to predict returns. The second and more crucial step explores the economic mechanisms underlying return predictions. Without the second step, the initial results would remain a black box.

Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)first estimate the SDF at a 15-minute horizon using a method adapted fromChen, Pelger, and Zhu (2024), who use neural networks to approximate the SDF’s unknown functional form at a monthly horizon.Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)apply this method to 15-minute returns and generate a high-frequency SDF measure. Not surprisingly, the high-frequency SDF returns have a 23.9% correlation with the high-frequency returns on the aggregate market portfolio. Moreover, the high-frequency SDF does a much better job explaining the cross-sectional variation in the 272 high-frequency test portfolios than the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) and theFama and French (2018)six-factor model, asAleti and Bollerslev’s (2025)SDF estimator aims to minimize the largest possible squared pricing errors.

Next,Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)identify returns exceeding three standard deviations as jumps and link these jumps with real-time economic news and corresponding news topics. They find that the SDF rarely jumps without identifiable news. This result seems to contrast directly with the widely cited study ofCutler, Poterba, and Summers (1989), who find that very few cases among the 50 largest daily changes in the S&P 500 can be explained by news in the next day’sNew York Times. The differences, however, can be easily attributed to two advancements in big data.

First,Cutler, Poterba, and Summers (1989)specified a 24-hour time window during which many events could happen. The availability of tick-by-tick data now allows researchers to construct high-frequency returns.Fair (2002)found that 53/69 events can be identified using minute-level return data. Second, the availability of machine-readable news data not only expands news coverage but also allows researchers to identify the correct timestamp of the news. The availability of high-frequency news further helps link high-frequency returns with relevant news.

Finally,Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)attribute jumps in SDF to different news topics. They find that monetary policy and finance-related news account for most SDF variation. Interestingly, much of the priced monetary policy news is not covered by traditional monetary policy calendars. Their paper’s Internet Appendix provides examples of “missing” non-calendar news, such as unscheduled central bank communications and speeches by Federal Reserve officials. This emphasis on unscheduled news echoesChinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019), who highlight the importance of unscheduled news about firm fundamentals for individual stock returns at the minute level.

1.2.2. Two methods to impute missing data

Freyberger et al. (2025)propose a conditional mean approach, which assumes that data are missing at random after controlling for 13 characteristics that are always observed, including size, book-to-market, beta, idiosyncratic risk, and the previous month’s return. To impute a missing characteristiclof stocki, they rely on all stocks for which all characteristics are observed. On this subset of the data, they first regress characteristiclon the observed characteristics of stocki(which may differ among stocks) and then use the estimated coefficients multiplied by these observed characteristics for stockito impute the missingC|${}_{\textit{il}}$|⁠.They then use generalized least squares (GLS) to make return predictions, placing less weight on observations with imputed characteristics due to the additional imputation error.

Bryzgalova et al. (2025)assume characteristics follow a factor structure, where characteristicC|${}_{\textit{il}}$|is the product of a stockspecific partF|${}_{\textit{i}}$|and a characteristic-specific part|$\Lambda_{\textit{l}}$|plus an error term—that is,|$C_{il}=F_{i}\Lambda_{l}+e_{i,l}$|⁠. The core of their method is to apply a version of principal component analysis (PCA) to the covariance matrix of characteristics when some data are missing. Here, we offer an intuitive explanation of their method.

We anticipate that most RFS readers have some teaching experience. Suppose your course includes grades from several homework assignments and two exams. During the pandemic, some students might not have submitted all assignments or attended all exams due to COVID-19. If you give your course spreadsheet toBryzgalova et al. (2025)and ask them to impute the missing grades, their first step is constructing a covariance matrix with missing data. For instance, if 90 out of 100 students submitted both Homework 1 and Homework 2, they use the covariance of these 90 students to input the covariance between Homework 1 and Homework 2. Similarly, other covariance matrix elements use pairwise covariance between observed characteristics.

Next,Bryzgalova et al. (2025)apply PCA to the imputed covariance matrix to generate imputed values for missing variables. Intuitively, the imputed values are weighted averages of observed characteristics, with weights depending on the correlation between two characteristics in the imputed covariance matrix. For example, if the correlation between midterm and final exam scores is high, the imputed midterm score will weigh the final exam score heavily. The PCA method inBryzgalova et al. (2025)is intuitively similar, likely identifying a distinct factor closely related to homework scores and another closely related to exam scores. Once they impose sparsity on the number of nonzero weights (Section 4.3 in Bryzgalova2025), their data-driven approach likely yields rules akin to our real decisions. If a student misses one exam, we would likely fill in the missing score using their score from another exam and ignore or significantly downweigh the information from their homework scores.

These two papers on missing data are complementary.Bryzgalova et al. (2025)use a factor structure for the characteristics, whileFreyberger et al. (2025)use conditional means to impute missing values. The papers also differ in the prediction step. InBryzgalova et al. (2025), imputed and observed characteristics receive equal weight, butFreyberger et al. (2025)can easily down-weight imputed variables using GLS. As imputing characteristics is an intermediate step for both papers’ final goal to predict monthly returns, the more efficient weighting inFreyberger et al. (2025)implies that their mean square errors (MSE) for coefficients can be smaller even when the MSE of imputed variables is larger (see Table 3 inFreyberger et al. 2025). Moreover,Freyberger et al. (2025)show how to adjust standard errors to take the imputation step into account. We also witness the complementarity of these two papers in the editorial process. Table 3 inFreyberger et al. (2025)shows that their imputed variables outperform those inBryzgalova et al. (2025)when the variables do not follow a strong factor structure. Here, we offer some inside information: although both papers appear in the same special issue,Freyberger et al. (2025)was accepted earlier thanBryzgalova et al. (2025). Therefore,Bryzgalova et al. (2025)address the weak factor issue in their final version using ridge regressions. Therefore, each paper gives its complementary paper information for new discoveries. We conjecture that a promising new research direction is to combine the factor structure inBryzgalova et al. (2025)with the weighting scheme inFreyberger et al. (2025)to develop an even better estimator.

1.3. Unstructured data

The first step in analyzing unstructured data is usually to transform them into structured data. Researchers can transform the data themselves, sometimes with the help of experts from other fields. However, this approach often does not work because finance researchers may lack the expertise to transform the data or may not even be allowed to access the raw data. Therefore, a more efficient approach is often to use data transformed by other researchers or data vendors.Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)use both approaches, and their paper provides an excellent example of the strengths of each approach.

Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)find that earnings announcements from firms in higher-centrality counties generate stronger immediate price, volatility, and trading volume reactions. They use two unstructured datasets to measure a firm’s centrality. Their main measure comes from the Social Connectedness Index (SCI), which measures the connectedness between U.S. counties (Bailey et al. 2018) using data from Facebook. The centrality of a firm is measured as the centrality of its headquarters county in the matrix of SCIs between county pairs. The strength of the SCI from Facebook is its extensive reach and the relative representativeness of its user base. The weakness of the SCI is that it is not designed forHirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025). For example, the SCI they use captures a snapshot from 2016 and is at the county level, so there is no temporal variation. Therefore,Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)supplement SCI with StockTwits data, an unstructured data source that they transform into structured data themselves. They create time-varying measures of social network centrality based on StockTwits influencers. The dynamic nature of this data allows them to incorporate firm fixed effects, which helps control for latent, confounding factors tied to firm or county characteristics that might be associated with their Facebook-based measures.

Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)find that earnings announcements from firms in higher-centrality counties generate stronger immediate price and volatility reactions. Post-announcement, such firms experience weaker price drift and faster volatility decay. These results indicate that earnings news from more centrally located firms is more rapidly incorporated into stock prices. Therefore, network centrality is associated with greater diffusion of investor attention to earnings news and greater price efficiency.

Although the return pattern inHirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)is consistent with traditional models such asKarpoff (1986),Kim and Verrecchia (1991),Harris and Raviv (1993),Kim and Verrecchia (1994),Kandel and Pearson (1995),Scheinkman and Xiong (2003), andBanerjee and Kremer (2010), the volume dynamics go in the opposite direction. If higher connectedness in the social network accelerates information diffusion and if investors are fully rational, higher news centrality should be associated with faster decay in both volatility and volume.Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025), however, find that high centrality leads to more persistent trading volume.

Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)then propose a social churning hypothesis to rationalize the sharply contrasting dynamics of volatility and volume responses to the news. Although models with homogeneous beliefs would predict faster decay of volatility and volume for high-centrality firms, news diffusion may also change people’s beliefs. The social churning hypothesis conjectures that news centrality also triggers more intense discussion and causes persistent fluctuations in disagreement. If these idiosyncratic belief noises do not affect prices, an increase in news centrality increases immediate price jumps, reduces future price drift, and, at the same time, increases trading volume.

Because the ready-to-use Facebook data do not provide information on disagreements between Facebook friends, StockTwits data become essential to testing volume dynamics. The authors first measure the sentiment of each message through textual analysis and then measure the disagreement using the standard deviation of the sentiment. They find that earnings announcements by high-centrality stocks are associated with greater and more persistent disagreements, which in turn explains the persistent trading volume.

1.4. Valuing data

One open question posed byGoldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021)is, “What is the price of data?”Farboodi et al. (2025)take a significant step forward in addressing this important question by focusing on the financial industry and data demand. Despite these two simplifications, the authors face a daunting task because investors’ willingness to pay depends not only on their own characteristics but also those of other market participants, such as who else has access to the data.

Fortunately,Farboodi et al. (2025)demonstrate, using a rational expectations equilibrium framework, that other people’s characteristics and information sets have already been incorporated into the price at timetand do not forecast returns at timet+ 1. They propose that conditional variance is a sufficient statistic for valuing data. Their most important step comes from their regressions (12) and (13), which generate forecast errors without and with the new data. The flexibility of these two equations allows the authors to add more layers to data valuation.

Investment style:The authors evaluate data for different investment styles by limiting the set of risky assets. For example, the data value for a growth investor depends on the reduction in forecast error for growth stocks. The authors find that I/B/E/S data are more important for growth investors than value investors.

Previously purchased data:The authors incorporate previously purchased data by changing the variables they condition on. Their baseline regression uses the consumption-wealth ratio fromLettau and Ludvigson (2001)as a proxy for existing data. Then, they evaluate the sensitivity of data values to other sources of information by adding more conditional variables.

Investment horizon:The authors value the data for investors of different horizons by running the same regressions at different horizons.

Farboodi et al. (2025)show that the value of data depends on an investor’s wealth level and market liquidity. Investor wealth affects data value through risk aversion. If two investors have the same relative risk aversion, the wealthier investor has lower absolute risk aversion and, as a result, values the same data more highly. When trading does not have a price impact, the authors show that investor wealth can generate very steep demand curves because an investor with $250 million in initial wealth values data almost 300 times more than one with $0.5 million. However, a small decrease in market liquidity, measured by a small increase in price impact, can lead to large variations in the value of data and, consequently, in the valuation of firms whose main asset is financial data. In addition, price impact flattens the data demand curve, because it reduces the data value more for investors with higher data valuations.
article.2. Where Does Big Data Research Go from:
In the previous sections, we reviewed the seven papers in this special issue and related them to the discussion inGoldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021). Now, we discuss some new thoughts about promising lines of research.

2.1. The SEC’s four proposals for market structure reform

One exciting aspect of market microstructure research is its ability to assess existing policies and influence the development of new ones.Bartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025) closely examine the Regulation National Market System (Reg NMS) II, one of SEC chairman Gary Gensler’s four proposals introduced in December 2022. These four proposals offer two kinds of research opportunities. First, researchers can write papers to refine or reject the policies. Second, if implemented, the proposed policies would provide opportunities to evaluate the policies or use them as shocks to study other questions. Such studies often need to employ big data techniques, and so become more viable these days with the flourishing of big data research.

The least controversial proposal, titled “Disclosure of Order Execution Information,” was adopted in March 2024 but has not yet been implemented.Topbas and Ye (2024)provide conjectures on the impact of increased disclosure based on other transparency shocks, but the true impact of the policy cannot be known until its actual implementation.

While preparing the introduction’s final version, the SEC adopted the second proposal, “Tick Size and Access Fees and the Transparency of Better Priced Orders.” This proposal reduces the tick size from 1 cent to 0.5 cents for quotations and orders in NMS stocks priced at or greater than $1 per share if the time-weighted average spread of the stock during a specified three-month evaluation period is lower than 1.5 cents. This policy contrasts with the 2016 Tick Size Pilot Program, in which the SEC increased the tick size from 1 cent to 5 cents for 1,200 randomly selected small or medium stocks. The literature shows that the Tick Size Pilot Program affected variables beyond market microstructure. For instance,Albuquerque, Song, and Yao (2020)find that an increase in tick size raises the cost of capital, whileLi, Ye, and Zheng (2024)find that an increase in tick size significantly reduces share repurchases. It will be interesting to see whether a reduction in tick size leads to opposite results, particularly because the new policy is more likely to affect larger, more liquid stocks.

U.S. stock exchanges have complex fee structures, to the extent thatSpatt (2020)questioned whether these fee structures are anti-competitive. The second part of “Tick Size and Access Fees and the Transparency of Better Priced Orders” reduces the fee cap from 0.3 cents per share to 0.1 cents per share for protected quotations and other best bids and offers in NMS stocks priced at $1 or more. The policy also increases transparency by requiring exchanges to make all fees, rebates, and other forms of remuneration determinable at the time of execution. It will be interesting to examine whether and how changes in fee structures affect market quality and whether such impacts are consistent with the theoretical predictions of models on exchange fees, such as those byColliard and Foucault (2012),Foucault, Kadan, and Kandel (2013), andChao, Yao, and Ye (2019).

The third part of the proposal aims to reduce the lot size, which relates closely to the papers byBartlett, McCrary, and O’Hara (2025)in this special issue. It would be interesting to examine the impact of lot size reduction, which could go beyond market microstructure. For example,Li and Ye (2024)predict that a reduction in lot size may reduce firms’ incentives to split their stocks.

Da, Fang, and Lin (2025)show that fractional trading increases retail trading. This change, along with other market structure changes such as zero-commission trading, has led to a surge in retail trading. The increased importance of retail trading is one reason the SEC proposed a rule to enhance competition for individual investor order execution, focusing on imposing order-by-order competition for retail orders.Ernst, Spatt, and Sun (forthcoming)argue that order-by-order auctions, though allocatively efficient, reduce competition and increase adverse selection. Their findings raise questions about the optimal execution mechanism for retail orders.

“Regulation best execution” includes execution for retail traders, but also for institutional orders. One significant challenge is defining what constitutes best execution.Li, Ye, and Zheng (2023)find strong evidence that obtaining the best execution is not equivalent to just obtaining the best price. Fifty-seven percent of the trading volume on the NYSE comes from orders that specifically refuse to be routed to the NBBO. Therefore, defining what best execution actually is and evaluating different proposals for best execution raises many interesting questions.

2.2. Understanding investors who are slower than high-frequency traders but faster than traditional funds

Goldstein, Spatt, and Ye (2021)mentioned that one promising line of research is to understand investors who are slower than high-frequency traders but faster than traditional funds. Renaissance Technologies is an example of this category. When Renaissance’s founder passed away in May 2024, Clifford Asness, co-founder of AQR, mentioned, “There is one GOAT [greatest of all time]. His name was Jim Simons.”4Despite the increasing importance of computer-oriented quant traders, the academic finance literature still has a limited understanding of them. One reason is that many of these traders come from nonfinance backgrounds. The other reason is that their frequency is too fast for traditional asset pricing research and too slow for market microstructure research.Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)provide excellent insights into these missing frequencies, and there are certainly more opportunities in this underexplored territory.

First,Aleti and Bollerslev (2025)andChinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2019)find that unscheduled news plays a major role in return predictability at the minute-by-minute horizon. It would be interesting to examine other drivers of return predictability at this horizon. Second, as the quant traders operate at time scales ranging “anywhere from a few minutes to a few months,”5it would be interesting to study return predictability at other frequencies. Moving the literature from nanoseconds to minutes makes the gap smaller, but there is still a long way to bridge the gap if we do not know what happens at hourly or daily frequencies.

Missing data will be another interesting question for high-frequency return predictability. AlthoughAleti and Bollerslev (2025)calculate the high-frequency return, they construct their factor portfolio at a lower frequency. Therefore, they face a problem similar to the missing characteristics problem in the papers dealing with lower-frequency returns. The two methods to impute missing data proposed in this special issue may helpAleti and Bollerslev (2025)improve their results. Also, as the data on stock characteristics are updated at different frequencies, missing data may appear in the frequency domain: researchers may use stale information for characteristics updated less frequently. One way to overcome issues with stale information is to use unstructured data. For example,Zhu (2019)discusses the use of records of consumer transactions and satellite imagery to get more frequent estimations of firm fundamentals. It would be interesting to see whether statistical methods can also impute more frequent estimators of characteristics from other characteristics updated at higher frequencies.

2.3. Missing not at random

Freyberger et al. (2025)andBryzgalova et al. (2025)both deal with data missing at random after controlling for observed characteristics. An interesting line of research would address cases where the probability that a particular observation is missing depends on missing variables even after controlling for observables (missing not at random, or MNAR for short). For example, the Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) rating is likely missing if a firm does not voluntarily disclose its ESG performance on time.6Such voluntary disclosure decisions may depend on various unobservable factors, such as the firm’s expectations regarding market sentiments on ESG performance and the manager’s preference toward ESG policies. The issue can be difficult to tackle when the coverage criteria employed by different ESG rating providers are not transparent. For example, the ESG rating may be more likely to be missing if it has more extreme values, a problem that may not be fully resolved after we control for observed characteristics or factor structure.

When MNAR arises, the process generating the missing values has to be modeled explicitly. Chapter 15 ofLittle and Rubin (2019)introduces a variety of different statistical methods to deal with MNAR, but it is our job to choose and defend the assumptions of these methods based on our institutional knowledge of finance. One analogy is the instrumental variable approach. Finance researchers did not invent the instrumental variable approach, but we need to defend the assumptions of the instrumental approach using our knowledge of economics and finance in any application. Therefore, machine learning would not replace our domain knowledge; instead, they complement one another.

2.4. More complex data

Hirshleifer, Peng, and Wang (2025)show the importance of unstructured data and the difficulty of analyzing them directly. Fortunately, more data vendors are joining the industry, helping us transform unstructured data into structured data. The bookBig Data and AI Strategies, which we cited in the introduction to the first special issue, now has its second edition.7The new edition offers a more extensive list of alternative data vendors than the first.Denev and Amen (2020)also provide an excellent survey of the alternative data landscape.

More complex data create value if they capture new information not present in simpler data. For example, one way to analyze speech data is to transcribe it into text. However, simplification may cause the loss of the most important information because it is easier to detect deception if we have audio and video information. Once we detect deception, we may ignore the information in the transcript or interpret the information in the transcript in the opposite direction.

2.5. Open question for valuing data

Farboodi et al. (2025)estimate the demand curve for financial data. This raises several natural questions. The first is valuing the supply of data. Global big data technology is valued at USD 349.40 billion in 2023 and is projected to grow from $397.27 billion in 2024 to $1,194.35 billion by 2032.8Therefore, it is important to study the supply side of big data and, more importantly, how supply and demand interact to determine the equilibrium price of the data.

Farboodi et al. (2025)value financial data. It would be interesting to study the value of data for nonfinancial firms.Veldkamp (2023)proposes six approaches to value data, but the literature is still very sparse. One notable exception isWernerfelt et al. (2022), who estimate the value of data to advertisers on Meta.

Finally,Farboodi et al. (2025)estimate the private value of the data for investors. It would be interesting to study the value of data for society.Hirshleifer (1971)models two types of information: (i) foreknowledge of states of the world that will be revealed by Nature itself (for example, earnings announcements), and (ii) discovery of hidden properties of Nature that can only be revealed through action. If big data mainly creates foreknowledge but not discovery, traders who acquire the data may have substantial financial benefits, but the benefit for society is zero or negative after adjusting for the cost of creating the data. Additionally, while big data may increase price efficiency by incorporating more information into prices, it is not clear whether big data increases real efficiency (Bond, Edmans, and Goldstein 2012).Zhu (2019)shows that consumer transactions and satellite images increase both price and real efficiency. It would be interesting to see whether her results hold in other scenarios.
article.3. Conclusion: This RFS special issue on “The Next Chapter in Big Data in Finance” showcases the significant progress in our field since the first NBER/RFS conference on big data and the special issue that followed it. Several of the questions raised in the introduction to our previous special issue have been touched on in the new papers, and other papers have sprung up following recent developments in the market. We have provided some thoughts about where future research can go, and we generally look forward to seeing new research tackling these issues and other emerging questions for the next chapter of big data research in finance.
